nutrient_source.title: Beef, stir-fried, with vegetables (adapted)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Beef, stir-fried, with vegetables' (FDC ID 170068), adjusted for Marmite, honey, and soy sauce content. Marmite adds sodium and B vitamins, honey adds sugar, and soy sauce adds sodium.

gi_source.title: Honey (analogous estimate)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Glycemic index estimated based on the presence of honey and other sugars in the sauce, and starch thickeners. Honey typically has a GI of 60-70. Beef itself has a GI of 0. The overall dish is estimated to be medium GI.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from Marmite and soy sauce.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume Marmite Beef in moderation due to its high sodium content, which can contribute to elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar and honey in the sauce, and potential starch thickeners.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the sugar and carbohydrate content in Marmite Beef, especially if served with rice, as it can impact blood glucose levels.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from beef and high sodium content.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular health, it is advisable to consume Marmite Beef in moderation, opting for leaner cuts of beef and being aware of the overall sodium intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marmite Beef is a popular Chinese-Singaporean zi char (cooked food stall) dish featuring tender slices of beef stir-fried in a distinctive savory-sweet sauce. The sauce is primarily made with Marmite, a concentrated yeast extract known for its unique umami and salty flavor, balanced with honey or sugar, soy sauce, garlic, and ginger, often thickened with cornstarch. The beef is typically marinated and quickly stir-fried to retain its tenderness, then coated in the rich, glossy sauce. The dish offers a complex flavor profile that is both savory and slightly sweet, with a characteristic malty undertone from the Marmite. Nutritionally, it is a good source of protein from the beef, but can be high in sodium due to Marmite and soy sauce, and moderate in sugar from the honey. It is often served with steamed white rice.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from a similar USDA entry and adjusted for specific ingredients. GI is an analogous estimate based on sauce components. Multilingual names and ontology are well-researched.
